---
title: General Team Info
---

# General Information:

Location: Nacogdoches, Texas. 
Conference: CUSA
FBS Inagural Year: 2026-2027

Important Note: In real life Stepen F. Austin University is in this same location, however in our fictional universe they do not exist at all. Caddo State exists there instead. 

Additionally, since gameplay takes place in EA Sports' College Football 27 Dynasty mode, I'm required to replace a team. I have chosen to replace Sam Houston from the Conference USA, at the start of our Dynasty, Caddo State will play in CUSA.


# Background

Prior to the 2026 season, Caddo State was a regional nobody, a middling D3 program that had seen a neglected football team struggle in the ranks of Division 3 football. For decades the Lumberjacks were an afterthought even within their own athletic department — a program defined by aging facilities, shoestring budgets, and a revolving door of coaches who treated Nacogdoches as a stepping stone rather than a destination. Winning seasons were rare, postseason appearances rarer still, and the program's ceiling seemed permanently fixed.
All of that changed in the 2020's as talk of NIL and investment entered the college realm for the first time. Where most saw disruption, a handful of wealthy Caddo State boosters saw a rare market inefficiency: a full four-year institution with an established campus, a football-hungry East Texas fanbase, and an enrollment base large enough to sustain a major program — all sitting dormant at the D3 level. The pieces were already there. What the program had never had was capital.
Several of those boosters decided to change that, and they did so aggressively. Rather than trickle money into the program, they committed to building a contender from the ground up — brick by brick, dollar by dollar. By 2024 the investment had transformed the program almost beyond recognition: major stadium renovations modernized a facility that had gone untouched for a generation, a complete coaching and roster overhaul brought in legitimate talent and staff, and the Lumberjacks completed a rapid ascent to the FCS ranks. What had taken most programs half a century of gradual growth, Caddo State compressed into a few short years of concentrated ambition.
The investment paid off faster than anyone outside Nacogdoches expected. The Lumberjacks did not simply survive the jump to FCS — they thrived, quickly establishing themselves as a legitimate threat rather than a curiosity. That momentum culminated in a controversial 2025 season, one that saw Caddo State push all the way to the FCS playoff semifinal before falling to eventual national champion North Dakota State. It was a defeat, but a defining one: the Lumberjacks had gone toe-to-toe with the gold standard of FCS football and come up a single game short of the title game, announcing to the rest of the country that the program's rise was no fluke.
In the aftermath, both programs were admitted to the FBS. North Dakota State joined the Mountain West as a football-only member, while Caddo State accepted an invitation to Conference USA, also as a football-only member. For a program that had been a Division 3 nobody less than half a decade earlier, the ascent was staggering — and for the boosters who had bankrolled it, only the beginning. Caddo State enters the 2026 season with the facilities, the resources, and the ambition of a program with no intention of being anyone's easy win.


# Fanbase 

Caddo State's fanbase is defined by a peculiar duality — a program with the deepest roots in Texas and the shallowest history in major college football. Nacogdoches has been a community since long before Texas was a state, and that heritage runs through everything the Lumberjacks do. But the football program the rest of the country now sees is barely a decade removed from Division 3 obscurity, built and branded almost overnight. The result is a fanbase still figuring out exactly what it is.
At its core sit the locals — the East Texas families who backed the Lumberjacks when the stands were half-empty and the wins were rare. To them, Caddo State was always Nacogdoches' team long before the money arrived, and they carry a quiet pride in having been there first. This old guard gives the fanbase its authentic Piney Woods character: blue-collar, tradition-minded, and fiercely protective of the idea that Caddo State represents East Texas rather than any one booster's checkbook.
Layered on top is the new wave — students, alumni, and regional fans drawn in by the program's meteoric rise. The overnight success brought an influx of energy, attendance, and expectation that the program had never known. These fans arrived for the winning, and they expect it to continue. Their presence transformed a sleepy D3 crowd into a genuine home-field atmosphere, but it also raised the stakes: this is a fanbase that has only ever known ascension, and has little patience for a step backward.
The friction between the two — heritage versus ambition, the built-in versus the bought-in — is precisely what gives the Caddo State fanbase its edge. Rivals are quick to dismiss the Lumberjacks as a manufactured program, a team that "bought" its way up while more storied schools climbed the hard way. Caddo State fans have learned to wear that criticism as a badge. The chip on the shoulder is real, and it has become part of the program's identity: the sense that no matter how far the Lumberjacks rise, they will always have something to prove.
If there is one thing that unites the whole fanbase, it is the axe. The imagery of the Lumberjack — rugged, relentless, built by hard work — resonates equally with the old timber-country locals and the new-money believers. It lets both halves of the fanbase tell the same story about themselves: that Caddo State was carved out of nothing, swing by swing, and that the program is only getting started.

## Gameday

Founders Field. The Lumberjacks play in Founders Field, a name that carries a deliberate double meaning in Nacogdoches. To longtime locals, it honors the founders of the oldest town in Texas; to the modern program, it nods to the boosters who founded this era of Caddo State football. The renovated stadium is the physical embodiment of the program's rise — a facility rebuilt from the ground up, now one of the loudest and most modern venues in Conference USA. Its navy-and-gold seating bowl and towering video boards stand in stark contrast to the aging bleachers the program played in barely a decade ago.
The First Chop. Caddo State's signature pregame tradition. Before every home game, an honorary "founder" — a booster, a distinguished alum, or a local legend — takes a ceremonial swing of a gold-bladed axe into a section of timber at midfield as the crowd counts down. The first chop signals that Founders Field is open for business, and the stadium erupts as the team takes the field. It's equal parts old-timber heritage and new-money spectacle, which is exactly the point.
The Axe Wall. As the Lumberjacks storm the tunnel, players slap a mounted double-bladed axe — a nod to the swing-by-swing, brick-by-brick ethos the program was built on. Every man touches iron before he touches the field.
The Woodpile. Caddo State's student section, packed into the end zone at Founders Field. Loud, young, and drawn in by the program's meteoric rise, the Woodpile is the beating heart of the new-wave fanbase — the section that turned a sleepy D3 crowd into a genuine home-field advantage. They arrive early, stay standing, and take personal offense at any suggestion that the Lumberjacks "bought" their way up.
Navy Out / Gold Out. Depending on the opponent and the uniform set, Founders Field organizes full-stadium color themes — a wall of navy for marquee matchups, a blinding gold out for rivalry and primetime games. On the biggest nights, the goldout paired with the Lumberjacks' own navy-and-gold sets turns the whole venue into a single, unbroken color.
The Founders' Torch. A running nod to the program's identity: a torch (or brazier) lit at the stadium's entrance before kickoff and kept burning through the fourth quarter — a symbol that the program, once left for dead, has been permanently rekindled. Extinguished only after a Lumberjack victory.

## Mascot

"Gil" the Lumberjack. Caddo State's mascot is Gil, a towering, broad-shouldered lumberjack in navy flannel and a gold-bladed axe slung over one shoulder. His name is a nod to Antonio Gil Y'Barbo, the historical founder of Nacogdoches — a deliberate link between the oldest town in Texas and the program that now carries its name. In the fiction of Caddo State, Gil is the spirit of the founder reborn as the embodiment of the football program: a figure who carved something out of the East Texas timber where there was once nothing at all.
His backstory mirrors the program's own. As the legend goes, Gil was the first man to swing an axe in the Piney Woods that would become Nacogdoches — clearing the land, laying the first timbers, and building a home out of raw wilderness. Centuries later, when a forgotten football program needed to be built from the ground up, Gil returned to do what he does best: build. Every ceremonial First Chop at Founders Field is framed as Gil's work, and the "brick by brick, dollar by dollar" ethos of the modern program is treated in-universe as simply the latest chapter of a job he started 250 years ago.
On game days, Gil patrols the sideline and leads the Woodpile in the "Timber!" call, raising his axe overhead before every fourth-down stand. Rugged, relentless, and impossible to intimidate, he's the perfect avatar for a fanbase that prides itself on having been carved out of nothing.
